structured.summary: This paper proposes a novel framework for generating expressive and intuitive individual fairness specifications for text classification tasks, particularly toxicity classification. It leverages unsupervised style transfer, GPT-3 zero-shot capabilities, and human feedback via crowdsourcing to generate, validate, and learn similarity functions that align with human fairness intuitions, and demonstrates the utility of these learned specifications in training fairness-aware classifiers.

strengths.point: Innovative combination of methods for generating fairness constraints
strengths.evidence: The paper combines word replacement, unsupervised style transfer, and GPT-3 zero-shot editing to generate diverse candidate pairs for fairness constraints (Section 3.1).

strengths.point: Use of human feedback and active learning to align fairness specifications with human intuition
strengths.evidence: The authors conduct extensive crowdsourcing studies to validate candidate pairs and train a BERT-based similarity classifier using active learning to approximate human fairness judgments (Section 3.2, Section 4.4).

strengths.point: Demonstrated improvement in fairness-aware downstream classification
strengths.evidence: Experiments show that classifiers trained with the generated and filtered constraints achieve better adherence to human-validated fairness constraints in toxicity classification (Section 4.5).

strengths.point: Comprehensive experimental evaluation and ethical considerations
strengths.evidence: The paper includes detailed experiments on diversity, relevance, learning similarity functions, and downstream classifier performance, along with an ethics statement addressing human evaluation and limitations (Sections 4 and 6).

weaknesses.point: Limited generalization of learned similarity function
weaknesses.evidence: The learned similarity classifier ˆφ achieves only moderate balanced accuracy (~66.7%) and shows limited improvement when filtering constraints for downstream training (Section 4.4, 4.5).

weaknesses.point: Disagreement among annotators on fairness judgments
weaknesses.evidence: Substantial disagreement exists between crowdworkers on which pairs should be treated similarly, complicating the learning and evaluation of the similarity function (Section 4.3, Ethics Statement).

weaknesses.point: Evaluation focused primarily on toxicity classification
weaknesses.evidence: While the framework is general, experiments and human evaluations are conducted only on toxicity classification, limiting evidence of applicability to other NLP tasks (Section 4.1).
Crowdsourcing workers are US-based Mechanical Turk participants who may not be direct stakeholders or experts, which may bias fairness judgments (Section 6).

weaknesses.point: Dependence on pretrained models and external APIs
weaknesses.evidence: The approach relies on GPT-3 API and pretrained models like BERT and RoBERTa, which may limit reproducibility and introduce external biases (Section 3.1, Appendix B).

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on computational cost and scalability
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not provide detailed analysis of computational resources required for generating pairs, human labeling, and training similarity classifiers, which may impact practical deployment (Sections 3 and 4).

weaknesses.point: No formal theoretical guarantees on fairness improvement
weaknesses.evidence: While empirical results are promising, the paper lacks formal guarantees or bounds on how well the learned similarity function approximates true fairness notions (Section 3.2, Appendix C).

weaknesses.point: Filtering with learned similarity function shows limited benefit
weaknesses.evidence: Filtering constraints using the learned similarity function ˆφ did not clearly improve downstream classifier fairness or accuracy in some experiments (Section 4.5).

questions.question: How sensitive is the learned similarity function ˆφ to the choice of crowdworker population and annotation instructions?
questions.motivation: Understanding this sensitivity is important to assess robustness and fairness across different cultural or stakeholder groups.

questions.question: Can the proposed framework be effectively adapted to other NLP classification tasks beyond toxicity, such as sentiment analysis or hiring recommendation?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating generality would strengthen the impact and applicability of the approach.

questions.question: What are the computational and cost implications of generating large candidate sets and collecting human annotations at scale?
questions.motivation: Practical deployment requires understanding resource requirements and potential bottlenecks.

questions.question: Have the authors considered integrating expert or stakeholder feedback alongside crowdworker judgments to improve the quality of the learned similarity function?
questions.motivation: Expert input may reduce annotation noise and better capture domain-specific fairness notions.

critique_points.point_001: Limited generalization of learned similarity function (The learned similarity classifier ˆφ achieves only moderate balanced accuracy (~66.7%) and shows limited improvement when filtering constraints for downstream training (Section 4.4, 4.5).)
critique_points.point_002: Disagreement among annotators on fairness judgments (Substantial disagreement exists between crowdworkers on which pairs should be treated similarly, complicating the learning and evaluation of the similarity function (Section 4.3, Ethics Statement).)
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation focused primarily on toxicity classification (While the framework is general, experiments and human evaluations are conducted only on toxicity classification, limiting evidence of applicability to other NLP tasks (Section 4.1).)
critique_points.point_004: Potential biases in human feedback (Crowdsourcing workers are US-based Mechanical Turk participants who may not be direct stakeholders or experts, which may bias fairness judgments (Section 6).)
critique_points.point_005: Dependence on pretrained models and external APIs (The approach relies on GPT-3 API and pretrained models like BERT and RoBERTa, which may limit reproducibility and introduce external biases (Section 3.1, Appendix B).)
critique_points.point_006: Limited discussion on computational cost and scalability (The paper does not provide detailed analysis of computational resources required for generating pairs, human labeling, and training similarity classifiers, which may impact practical deployment (Sections 3 and 4).)
critique_points.point_007: No formal theoretical guarantees on fairness improvement (While empirical results are promising, the paper lacks formal guarantees or bounds on how well the learned similarity function approximates true fairness notions (Section 3.2, Appendix C).)
critique_points.point_008: Filtering with learned similarity function shows limited benefit (Filtering constraints using the learned similarity function ˆφ did not clearly improve downstream classifier fairness or accuracy in some experiments (Section 4.5).)
critique_points.point_009: How sensitive is the learned similarity function ˆφ to the choice of crowdworker population and annotation instructions? (Understanding this sensitivity is important to assess robustness and fairness across different cultural or stakeholder groups.)
critique_points.point_010: Can the proposed framework be effectively adapted to other NLP classification tasks beyond toxicity, such as sentiment analysis or hiring recommendation? (Demonstrating generality would strengthen the impact and applicability of the approach.)
critique_points.point_011: What are the computational and cost implications of generating large candidate sets and collecting human annotations at scale? (Practical deployment requires understanding resource requirements and potential bottlenecks.)
critique_points.point_012: Have the authors considered integrating expert or stakeholder feedback alongside crowdworker judgments to improve the quality of the learned similarity function? (Expert input may reduce annotation noise and better capture domain-specific fairness notions.)
